pattern: An element can only ever trade places with values close enough to it, so grouping values into value-proximity clusters and handing each cluster's smallest values to its lowest original positions first produces the one lexicographically smallest arrangement.
hints: Swapping only cares about how close two values are, not where they currently sit, so think about which elements could ever reach each other through a chain of close-enough swaps, regardless of position.

Sort the values first: two values that are close enough to their neighbor in that sorted order belong to the same reachable cluster, and a gap bigger than the limit ends one cluster and starts the next.

Within one cluster, every original position that contributed a value to it can end up holding any of that cluster's values, so to make the array as small as possible, sort those original positions and hand out the cluster's smallest values to the lowest-numbered ones first.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two clusters formed by chained swaps
statement example, duplicate values inside one cluster
statement example, no pair is close enough to swap
single element is always its own answer
smallest possible limit leaves every element in its own cluster
all duplicate values must reproduce the same array, not crash on ties
values and limit near the maximum bound stay transitively connected
an already-sorted array where the whole array is one cluster

stress.description: random values at the maximum array length with a random limit
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    limit = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    return [nums, limit]
